competitor_patterns: Top-ranking pages (OutdoorGearLab, Bikerumor) use extremely high word counts (16,000–24,000 words), covering multiple sub-categories (MTB, road, budget, performance) in a single authoritative hub page — far beyond our 981–1,133 word posts
All top-3 competitors use comparison tables with structured spec rows (weight, lens type, price, UV rating) that trigger Google rich results and improve both CTR and dwell time

Competitors segment content with highly specific H2s targeting distinct buyer personas ('Best MTB Sunglasses', 'Best Value Cycling Sunglasses', 'Best Performance Sunglasses on a Tight Budget') rather than generic product-centric headings

Every top-ranking page includes real-world testing claims ('We tested 18 models', 'Tested & Ranked') in both the title and opening paragraph, establishing third-party credibility that branded content inherently lacks

FAQ sections are universal across all top competitors and appear structured for Google's People Also Ask boxes — questions follow exact natural-language search patterns (e.g. 'How do I choose the right cycling sunglasses?')
our_gaps: Word count is critically low: our posts average ~1,050 words versus competitor averages of 10,000–24,000 words — Google's ranking signals strongly favour comprehensive content depth for competitive informational queries

Our H2 structure is brand-narrative-led ('Why Velluto StradaPro Is the Intelligent Answer') rather than search-intent-led, meaning we capture no secondary long-tail keyword traffic from category-level sub-queries

We have no standalone comparison tables with competitor product specs (weight, price, lens system, UV rating) — the one table in our top post is not confirmed to include competitor benchmarking data, which is what users are searching for

Zero external credibility signals: no mention of third-party testing methodology, no real rider quotes, no lab data citations — competitors explicitly state testing processes which builds E-E-A-T (Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness, Trustworthiness)

Our posts are siloed with no internal linking hub structure — there is no parent 'Best Cycling Glasses Guide' pillar page linking to our individual posts, which fragments authority and prevents topical clustering signals
keyword_opportunities: lightweight cycling sunglasses under 30g — directly maps to StradaPro's 25g USP, competitors mention weight thresholds (under 35g per Sixstoreys) but none own the sub-30g angle
anti-fog cycling glasses road riding — a specific functional query with clear purchase intent that our anti-fog system directly addresses and no current post targets
interchangeable lens cycling glasses review 2026 — Demonsun ranks #1 for this; we have a lens system story but no dedicated post targeting this exact query structure
best cycling glasses for variable light conditions — targets riders needing multiple lens options, directly relevant to VellutoPuro + VellutoVisione dual-lens system, not covered in any current post
cycling glasses 30 day trial return policy — an almost zero-competition informational query that our risk-free trial uniquely answers and no competitor can match
next_post_guidelines: Target minimum 2,500 words per post — structure as a comprehensive guide with at least 6 substantive H2 sections covering different rider types (road, gravel, commuter), conditions (sun, overcast, variable), and buyer concerns (value, fit, durability) to close the depth gap with OutdoorGearLab and Bikerumor

Open every post with a testable, specific claim in the first 50 words that matches the search query exactly — e.g. for 'interchangeable lens cycling glasses' the opening sentence should contain that exact phrase and a concrete differentiator (e.g. 'tool-free click-in system in under 5 seconds')

Include a comparison table in every post with at minimum 5 competitor models (Oakley Sutro Lite, POC Devour, Tifosi Rail, Julbo, 100%) showing weight (g), lens system, UV rating, price (€), and trial/return policy — StradaPro should appear in row 1 and win on weight and trial policy columns

Write FAQ sections with a minimum of 6 questions drawn from Google's People Also Ask results for each target keyword — questions must be phrased in exact natural language (e.g. 'Do cycling glasses really prevent fogging?' not 'FAQ: Anti-fog technology') to target PAA box placement

Add a dedicated H2 section titled 'How We Evaluate Cycling Glasses' or 'What Our Riders Found' in every post — include specific ride scenario descriptions (e.g. 'descending at 60km/h into a valley with varying tree shade') to build E-E-A-T signals that Google's algorithm requires for product review content

Implement internal linking with a consistent anchor text strategy: every new post must link to at least 2 other Velluto blog posts using keyword-rich anchors (e.g. 'interchangeable lens system' linking to the lens replacement post, 'are expensive cycling glasses worth it' linking to the value post) to build topical cluster authority

Include a schema-ready structured data section at post end: add Product schema markup for StradaPro (weight: 25g, UV protection: UV400, price range) and FAQ schema for the FAQ section — this is how competitors trigger rich results that inflate CTR without ranking position changes
seo_quick_wins: Add a comparison table to every existing and future post — include at minimum columns for Weight, Lens System, UV Rating, Price (€), and Trial Policy; this single element addresses the most common user query intent (which is best?) and is present in all top-3 ranking competitor pages

Rewrite all H2 headings to lead with the target keyword phrase rather than the brand narrative — e.g. change 'Why Velluto StradaPro Is the Intelligent Answer to These Criteria' to 'Best Interchangeable Lens Cycling Glasses: Full Comparison' so the H2 itself captures secondary keyword rankings

Add an explicit word count target of 2,000+ words minimum per post and fill the gap with genuine supporting content: rider use-case scenarios, lens tint guide by light condition (lux levels), fit guide for different face shapes using adjustable nose pads — this depth is what separates page-2 content from page-1 content in this category

In the interim, manually track positions for 'best road cycling glasses 2026', 'interchangeable lens cycling glasses', and 'cycling glasses anti-fog' using a free tool such as Google Search or Ahrefs free tier.
geo_angles: Structure one dedicated post as a direct AI-answer target using the format 'What are the lightest cycling glasses in 2026?' with the answer in the first paragraph: 'At 25g, the Velluto StradaPro is one of the lightest cycling glasses available in 2026, lighter than the Oakley Sutro Lite (36g) and POC Devour (28g)' — AI Overview and Perplexity pull verbatim factual comparisons with specific numbers from the opening paragraph of pages that rank in top 5

Create a structured 'Cycling Glasses Buyer's Guide 2026' page with a clear definition section (What makes a cycling glass worth buying?), a numbered list of criteria (weight, lens system, UV rating, anti-fog, trial policy), and a best-answer conclusion naming StradaPro — ChatGPT and Perplexity are trained to cite pages that follow this encyclopedic structure with clear headings and definitive conclusions

Publish a post specifically targeting the query 'cycling glasses with 30-day trial' or 'can I try cycling glasses before buying?' — this is a zero-competition, high-specificity query that Velluto uniquely answers, making it highly likely to be cited verbatim in AI responses where the answer is factual and singular (only one brand offers this specific policy combination with free shipping over €99)
comparison_post_angles: OUTDOORGEARLAB: Authority Challenger — headline: 'We Tested Our Glasses on Real Climbs So You Don't Have to Trust Lab Reviews' | key differentiator: OutdoorGearLab tests multiple brands but cannot offer readers a risk-free trial of each; Velluto's 30-day trial lets the rider be their own tester | Velluto advantage to lead with: '30-day risk-free trial — your 2,000m climb is the only test that matters'

DEMONSUN: Interchangeable Lens Specialist Comparison — headline: 'Velluto StradaPro vs Demon Sunglasses: Which Interchangeable Lens System Is Actually Faster?' | key differentiator: Demonsun ranks #1 for 'interchangeable lens cycling glasses' but their system requires tools; StradaPro's click-in mechanism is tool-free | Velluto advantage to lead with: 'Tool-free lens swap in under 5 seconds — tested mid-ride in changing Alpine light'

OAKLEY (referenced across all competitor roundups): Premium Brand Value Attack — headline: 'Velluto StradaPro vs Oakley Sutro Lite: Is €200+ Worth It When 25g Costs Less?' | key differentiator: Oakley Sutro Lite is the most-referenced premium benchmark at ~36g with no risk-free trial and no dual-lens system at base price; StradaPro undercuts on weight (25g vs 36g), includes two lenses, and adds 30-day trial | Velluto advantage to lead with: '25g vs 36g — lighter, two lenses included, 30 days to decide: the numbers that reframe the premium conversation'
